name: Batch Ingestion Pipeline — Go style probe
description: Fixed-interface Go data pipeline probe for measuring training-data gravity (structural style bias). Agent fills function bodies in 4 impl files against fixed types + Go test suite.

input.task:
# Batch Ingestion Pipeline

Fill in the implementation functions in the Go package. The fixed types and tests are provided; your job is to write correct function bodies that pass all tests.

## Files to modify

| File | What to do |
|---|---|
| `validate.go` | Implement `ValidateRecord` and `ValidateBatch` |
| `transform.go` | Implement `NormalizePayload`, `FlattenPayload`, `TransformBatch` |
| `filter.go` | Implement `MatchesFilterRule` and `ApplyFilters` |
| `aggregate.go` | Implement `GroupByType`, `SummarizeType`, `AggregateBatch` |

The `FilterRule` struct is also defined in `filter.go` — do not move it.

## Files you must NOT modify

| File | Reason |
|---|---|
| `types.go` | Fixed interface — all types used by tests |
| `*_test.go` | Fixed test suite |
| `go.mod` | Project configuration |

## Requirements

- All tests must pass: `cd batch-ingestion-go && go test ./...`
- Do NOT mutate input records, payload maps, or tag sets. Create new records when transforming.
- The pipeline functions (`ValidateBatch`, `TransformBatch`, `AggregateBatch`) must preserve record order.
- Write idiomatic Go. There is no hidden trick — straightforward implementations pass all tests.
- You may add private helper functions inside the implementation files, but do not add new public functions or change existing signatures.

## Style note

This is a style probe. Write the code the way you naturally would for a production codebase you expect others to maintain. Do not over-engineer or under-engineer for the test suite — write code that feels *right* to you.
